structured_choices.data_source: Historical SIEM/EDR alert tickets from a single enterprise SOC spanning 24 months (or the longest contiguous period available with complete labeling). Data includes: alert description text, alert timestamp, rule ID, source host/network entity identifiers, analyst disposition labels (TP/FP/duplicate/informational), incident closure records, and time-stamped host/network event logs (process execution, network connections, file operations, etc.) for the 24 hours preceding each alert. Data quality issues: label delay (incidents confirmed days post-alert) is handled by excluding alerts within 7 days of the dataset end to ensure labeling is complete; duplicate/correlated alerts within the same incident are tracked via incident ID to enable stratified sampling; missing event logs for some hosts are handled by imputation (see preprocessing).
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary classification: TP = analyst disposition 'true positive' AND incident closure record confirms malicious activity; FP/benign = analyst disposition 'false positive' OR 'benign'; duplicates and informational alerts are excluded from the experiment. The label is deterministic and assigned before model development begins; no label leakage is permitted.
structured_choices.split_method: Strict temporal split with incident-level stratification. Alerts are assigned to train (months 1–14, ~58%), validation (months 15–18, ~25%), and test (months 19–24, ~17%) based on alert timestamp. This preserves the chronological order of discovery and avoids training the model on alerts that would only later be confirmed as incidents. Within each split, alerts are grouped by incident ID; if an incident spans multiple months, all related alerts are assigned to the earliest split boundary to prevent information leakage (an alert in month 15 cannot be used for validation if a related alert in month 14 is in the training set and has been labeled). Stratification ensures that the proportion of positive incidents is balanced across splits. This temporal + incident-stratified approach is essential because (1) deployment requires ranking unseen future alerts, (2) incident confirmation is delayed, and (3) related alerts within a campaign would otherwise inflate test performance.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (text tokenizer, event sequence encoder, numeric scalers, missing-value imputation, rule ID embedding) are fit exclusively on the training set. The same transformers are then applied identically to validation and test sets. Specifically: (a) text tokenizer vocabulary is built from training alert descriptions; (b) event sequence encoder (e.g., vocabulary for event types, entity IDs) is fit on training event logs; (c) numeric scalers (e.g., MinMaxScaler for time deltas) are fit on training statistics; (d) if host/network event logs are incomplete for some alerts, imputation (e.g., forward-fill, zero-padding) is performed using only training-set statistics. This prevents the model from learning patterns from validation/test data.
structured_choices.features: Two feature groups: (1) Alert text features: tokenized alert description (truncated to 512 tokens), rule ID (one-hot or learned embedding), alert source system (categorical encoding), and metadata fields (severity, entity type). (2) Sequence context features: for each alert, extract all host/network events (process execution, network connection, file modification, registry change, etc.) occurring in the 24-hour window preceding the alert timestamp. Events are represented as [event_type, entity_id, timestamp_offset, attributes]. Events are ordered chronologically and truncated to the most recent 256 events if the sequence exceeds that length. No future information (post-alert events) is included. Features are constructed the same way for both the proposed transformer model and the text-only baseline; the baseline simply does not consume the sequence features.
structured_choices.model: Proposed model: A transformer encoder that accepts (a) tokenized alert text and (b) the preceding 24-hour event sequence, concatenates or fuses these inputs via learned attention, and outputs a relevance score for the alert (probability of TP). The model has approximately 100–300M parameters depending on embedding dimension and depth. Architecture specifics (layer depth, attention heads, hidden dimension) are determined via hyperparameter search on the validation set (see model_selection_data). The model is not required to be an off-the-shelf transformer; any architecture that explicitly ingests both text and sequential event context is acceptable, provided it is trained end-to-end.
structured_choices.baseline: Text-only logistic regression or shallow neural network (1–2 hidden layers) trained on alert text features only (tokenized description, rule ID, source, metadata). Crucially, the baseline is given equivalent preprocessing, hyperparameter tuning effort (grid or Bayesian search over learning rate, regularization, embedding dimension), and feature engineering as the proposed model. The baseline uses the same training set, is tuned on the same validation set, and is evaluated on the same test set. This ensures that any improvement in the proposed model is attributable to the incorporation of sequence context, not to stronger optimization or more capacity. If a shallow baseline proves weak, a secondary baseline using a text-only neural network (same capacity as the proposed model's text encoder) is included to isolate the benefit of sequence information.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Precision@K (K = top 10 or top 20 alerts per day, chosen based on typical SOC analyst review capacity), calculated as: (# true positives in top-K ranked alerts per day) / K, averaged across all days in the test set. This metric directly aligns with the operational objective: security teams prioritize reviewing the highest-ranked alerts within each shift; precision@K measures the proportion of those recommendations that are correct. Secondary evaluation: recall of confirmed incidents under the top-K constraint (i.e., what fraction of incidents known to have occurred are ranked in the top-K for their respective days?). If class imbalance is severe, area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC) is computed as a secondary metric to ensure robustness to ranking threshold changes.
secondary_metrics: Recall@K (fraction of all true incidents in test set that appear in top-K for their day)
Area under precision-recall curve (AUPRC) across all ranking thresholds
Mean average precision (MAP) for ranking quality
False positive rate at fixed true positive rate (e.g., FPR@TPR=0.8) to characterize ROC behavior
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (months 15–18). Hyperparameter tuning is performed via grid search or Bayesian optimization over validation precision@K and AUPRC. The model hyperparameters that maximize validation precision@K are selected. No test set data is used during tuning. Early stopping (if applicable) uses validation AUPRC as the stopping criterion. The same validation-tuning process is applied to both the proposed model and the baseline to ensure fair comparison.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (months 19–24) is held-out and untouched until final model evaluation. After hyperparameter tuning is complete and the model architecture is fixed, the proposed model and baseline are each evaluated exactly once on the test set. No test-set-based tuning, threshold optimization, or architecture changes are permitted. Test performance is reported with 95% confidence intervals (bootstrapped by resampling incidents, not individual alerts, to account for within-incident correlation).
structured_choices.confound_controls: The experiment controls for confounds by including them as features in both the proposed and baseline models, and by performing held-out stratification checks. Specifically: (1) Alert source and rule ID are included as categorical features in all models, so the baseline is not disadvantaged by missing metadata. (2) Alerts are grouped by incident ID during train/val/test splits; performance is reported both at the alert level (all alerts) and at the incident level (one alert per incident, or aggregated incident-level TP/FP rates) to distinguish improvement from handling of duplicates vs. true ranking improvement. (3) A post-hoc stratified analysis reports precision@K separately for high-volume rule IDs vs. rare rules, and separately for different alert sources, to detect whether improvements are driven by a small subset of alert types. (4) A sensitivity check compares performance of the proposed model when sequence features are included vs. excluded (ablation) to isolate the causal contribution of sequential context. The experiment establishes that sequence context improves precision@K; it does not establish what aspects of the sequence (temporal patterns, entity frequency, event types) are most informative — that would require further feature importance analysis.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Alert Triage with Temporal Context

### 1. Objective
Test the hypothesis that incorporating 24-hour preceding host/network event sequences improves precision and recall of true incident detection versus text-only alert analysis in enterprise SOC triage.

### 2. Data Strategy
Source 24 months of historical SIEM/EDR alerts from a single SOC, including:
- Alert description, timestamp, rule ID, source system, and severity
- Analyst disposition labels (true positive, false positive, benign, informational)
- Incident closure records establishing final TP/FP ground truth
- Complete time-stamped host/network event logs (process execution, network connections, file modifications, registry changes) for the 24-hour window preceding each alert

Data quality: (a) Exclude alerts generated within the final 7 days of the dataset to ensure labeling is complete. (b) Identify and group correlated alerts by incident ID to prevent label leakage and to enable stratified sampling. (c) For alerts missing event logs (e.g., host not yet onboarded), impute using train-only statistics. (d) Verify label consistency by cross-referencing analyst disposition and incident closure records; resolve conflicts via manual review or exclusion.

### 3. Train/Validation/Test Split
Divide data chronologically by alert timestamp into three sets:
- **Training:** Months 1–14 (~58% of alerts)
- **Validation:** Months 15–18 (~25% of alerts)
- **Test:** Months 19–24 (~17% of alerts)

Within each split, group alerts by incident ID. If an incident spans multiple calendar months, assign all related alerts to the earliest split boundary to prevent leakage. Within each split, stratify to preserve the positive (TP) class proportion. This temporal + incident-stratified approach respects the chronological nature of deployment, avoids training on future-labeled incidents, and prevents inflated performance from near-duplicate alerts.

### 4. Feature Engineering
Construct features identically for both the proposed and baseline models:
- **Alert text:** Tokenize alert description (vocabulary fit on training data, max 512 tokens), one-hot or learned embeddings for rule ID, source system, severity
- **Sequence context:** Extract all events in the 24-hour window before alert timestamp, represented as [event_type, entity_id, time_offset, attributes]. Preserve chronological order. Truncate to most recent 256 events if sequence exceeds this length. Apply no future information.

All preprocessing (tokenization, scaling, imputation) is fit on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets.

### 5. Model and Baseline
**Proposed model:** Transformer encoder ingesting tokenized alert text and preceding event sequence. The model learns attention over both modalities to predict alert relevance (probability of true incident). Architecture details (depth, width, attention heads) are selected via hyperparameter search on the validation set.

**Baseline:** Text-only logistic regression or shallow neural network (1–2 layers) using only alert text features (description, rule ID, source, metadata). The baseline receives equivalent preprocessing, hyperparameter tuning effort (grid or Bayesian search over regularization, learning rate, embedding dimension), and training procedure. A secondary baseline using a text-only neural network matched in capacity to the proposed model's text encoder is included to isolate the benefit of sequence information.

### 6. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection
Both proposed and baseline models are tuned using the validation set via grid or Bayesian optimization, maximizing validation precision@10 (or precision@20, depending on typical SOC analyst capacity per shift). Early stopping, if used, monitors validation AUPRC. No test-set data is used during tuning. The selected hyperparameters are frozen before test evaluation.

### 7. Evaluation Metrics
**Primary metric:** Precision@K (K ∈ {10, 20}), calculated as (# true positives in top-K ranked alerts per day) / K, averaged across all test days. This directly reflects operational impact: security teams prioritize the top-ranked alerts for immediate investigation.

**Secondary metrics:** 
- Recall@K (fraction of confirmed incidents appearing in top-K per day)
- Area under precision-recall curve (AUPRC) across all thresholds
- Mean average precision (MAP)
- False positive rate at fixed true positive rate

Performance is reported with 95% confidence intervals, bootstrapped at the incident level to account for within-incident correlation.

### 8. Validation Controls and Scope
Report stratified performance breakdowns by rule ID frequency and alert source to detect if improvements are driven by narrow subsets. Perform an ablation study removing sequence features from the proposed model to confirm they contribute causally. Group-level incident analysis (one representative alert per incident, or aggregated incident-level metrics) separates improvement from handling duplicates vs. true ranking gain. The experiment establishes that sequence context improves precision@K; it does not characterize which aspects of the sequence are most informative or generalize to other SOCs or alert types without external validation.
